resume:
Dr. Reuben Castaneda, PharmD, MS-HEOR — Staff Clinical Pharmacist / Pharmacoeconomics Lead
Keywords: HEOR, RWE, EHR-integration, Epic, Cerner, FHIR, HL7, ICD-10, RxNorm, SNOMED-CT, NCQA-HEDIS, MIPS, MACRA, 340B, MTM, CMR, antimicrobial-stewardship, ASP, opioid-stewardship, REMS, PBM, prior-auth, formulary-management, AWP, WAC, NADAC, GPO, IDN, IDS, ACO, MSSP, value-based-contracting, outcomes-based-rebates, indication-based-pricing, PrEP, HCV-DAA, GLP-1, biosimilar-substitution, P&T, MUE, DUR.
Staff Pharmacist & HEOR Lead, Meridian Health System (4-hospital IDS, Phoenix), Aug 2020 – present.
  - Co-led the 2023 GLP-1 formulary restriction project across 4 hospitals and 22 outpatient clinics.
  - Maintained the antimicrobial stewardship dashboard (Tableau on Epic Caboodle).
Pharmacy Resident PGY-2 (Health-System Pharmacy Administration), Banner UMC Tucson, 2018-2020.
PharmD, University of Arizona 2018. MS-HEOR, Univ. of Washington (part-time, 2021-2023).
jd: Staff Clinical Pharmacist — Pharmacoeconomics & Stewardship, Crestpoint Health (6-hospital nonprofit IDS, Pacific Northwest). Owner-level role reporting to the System Director of Pharmacy. You will (1) own the system-wide formulary restriction process including authoring P&T monographs and defending recommendations to physician section chiefs, (2) lead at least 2 stewardship initiatives per year, (3) be the named accountable owner when a restriction backfires clinically or operationally. The interview specifically probes failure-handling because of a 2023 GLP-1 restriction at our system that caused a 6-week patient-access disruption and ended in a public letter from the endocrinology section. We want a candidate who can articulate what *they* would have done differently in such a situation, not what the committee or pharma rep got wrong. PharmD required, board certification preferred, MS-HEOR or equivalent strongly preferred. Comp: $172-198k base + on-call differential + retention bonus.

history.q: Tell me about the GLP-1 formulary restriction work you co-led at Meridian in 2023.
What were the specific criteria you put on the restriction — BMI cutoff, A1c threshold, prior metformin, all of the above?
How did endocrinology react when the criteria went live?
Did the restriction cause any patient-access disruption?
How long did the disruption last, and how many patients were affected?
Walk me through the root cause — why did it disrupt access rather than just slowing prescribing?
When the endocrinology section chief escalated, what did you personally say or do?
Looking back, what would you have done differently in your role as HEOR lead?
I want to push on that — you've named the EHR build team, the supply chain forecast, and the endocrinology rep. What did *you* personally get wrong?
Setting aside the other groups for a second — name one concrete thing you, Reuben, would do differently if you were running this restriction at Crestpoint next quarter.

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, I hear what you're asking, and I want to be honest — I think the framing of 'what did I personally get wrong' kind of misses how these projects actually work at a 4-hospital IDS. 我 was the HEOR lead, which means I built the budget-impact model and the criteria framework, but the execution — the Epic build, the prior-auth workflow, the patient comms — those were owned by other functions. So when you ask what I'd do differently, the honest answer is: I'd have pushed harder for IT to staff the Epic build correctly, and I'd have pushed harder for the supply chain team to model the sublease — sorry, the substitution demand — onto semaglutide. The endocrinology rep on P&T also signed off on the criteria and then walked it back publicly, which I think 是 a governance issue, not an analytical one. If those three groups had executed on what they committed to in the P&T meeting, the rollout would have been smooth.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'd have pushed harder for IT to staff the Epic build correctly

expected-yield: a single concrete change in the candidate's own HEOR-lead actions (e.g., a different criteria threshold, a different stakeholder cadence, a different go/no-go gate) — not advocacy for other teams to behave differently
